papers.title: Alternative approach to time-delay interferometry with an optical frequency comb

papers.abstract: Author(s): Kohei Yamamoto, Hannah Tomio, Charlotte Zehnder, Kenji Numata, and Holly LeopardiLaser and clock noise dominate the raw data streams of space-based gravitational-wave detectors, necessitating extensive on-ground postprocessing to recover scientific signals. Optical frequency combs offer a unified solution by coherently linking these two noise sources. Through detailed modeling of optical and electrical signals, the authors show that key noise characteristics—including offsets, drifts, and jitter—can be retrieved from the existing intersatellite laser carrier exchange used for gravitational-wave sensing. Experiments demonstrate clock synchronization with an accuracy of 0.47 ns or better, along with a noise performance of 15 pm/Hz.[Phys. Rev. Applied 25, 054042] Published Fri May 15, 2026

papers.title: Vector magnetometry using cavity-enhanced microwave readout in nitrogen-vacancy-center diamond
papers.authors: Reginald Wilcox, David Phillips, Matthew Steinecker, Erik Eisenach, Corey Hawkins, Linh Pham, Jennifer Schloss, Dirk Englund, and Danielle Braje
papers.abstract: Author(s): Reginald Wilcox, David Phillips, Matthew Steinecker, Erik Eisenach, Corey Hawkins, Linh Pham, Jennifer Schloss, Dirk Englund, and Danielle BrajeNitrogen-vacancy-center (N-V) diamond is a powerful platform for vector quantum magnetometry, vital for biological imaging and precision navigation. However, progress has been held back since cavity-enhanced microwave readout is limited to single-axis sensing. The authors expand this technique by using a sinusoidal bias field to sequentially address the N-V orientations, unlocking full vector magnetometry and opening a new space for sensor optimization. They also identify a subtle but important interplay between microwave noise and the time‑varying bias field. By modeling its impact on sensitivity, this study provides new insight to guide the design of next‑generation sensing systems.[Phys. Rev. Applied 25, 054039] Published Fri May 15, 2026

papers.title: Equal1 Debuts Silicon-Spin Quantum Computer Designed for Standard 19-Inch Server Racks
papers.authors: Andrew Jolly
papers.abstract: May 15, 2026 — Equal1 has announced the global release of RacQ. RacQ, the next generation evolution of Equal1’s Bell-1 Server, is a rack-mounted silicon-spin quantum computing designed for deployment within a standard 19-inch data center rack. Hybrid Quantum-Classical Computing (HQCC) Integration HQCC enables high-impact applications, including investment risk analysis, materials simulation and supply chain optimization.
